assignment_description:
Listening to the Chorus Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
When we observe, we can use all our senses, but we often rely on sight more than our other senses. Use your time today to sit and just listen to the sounds of nature.

➜ LISTEN
Find a place to sit where you can hear the sounds of nature. Sit in a comfortable position, maybe with your eyes closed. Listen for about 7-10 mintues.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student heard.
• What sounds were the loudest? What sounds did you hear most often? Were there any that you had to listen very closely to hear? Do you know who you heard? Do you know where they live?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
If students seem anxious or unsure about their Nature Journals, teachers can encourage them by modeling, showing them many different examples, journaling in tandem, or serving as a scribe.

assignment_description:
Pond Habitat
⍞ Materials: Over and Under the Pond

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
Look at the picture on the cover. The creatures that we see and hear outside all have homes just like us! These homes are called habitats. Let's read a book today about one habitat, called a pond. If you have been to a pond, tell about or draw one thing that stood out to you. Did you ever think about a pond as someone's home?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Over and Under the Pond (whole book)
• If you have visited a pond before, who do you remember seeing there? If not, what or who do you hope to see when we visit?

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Prompt for General Science in Outdoor Work Quick Link.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Let students enjoy the artwork in this picture book. If you need to take a break and finish it another time, that is fine.

assignment_description:
Same Creatures, Different Homes Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

➜ INTRO
Some creatures live in only one particular habitat, while others live in many different habitats. Today let's notice creatures that live in many different habitats.

➜ OBSERVE
Spend about 10 minutes noticing creatures around home/school that also live at the pond. These could be insects, squirrels, and even trees or grasses.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Did you notice any creatures that live both at home/school AND at the pond? If so, how do you think their lives are different in these different habitats? If not, why do you suppose not?

Looking for Ants Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
Today, you will look for ants. Some good places to look are under small rocks, under loose bark, and in cracks.

➜ OBSERVE
Use a stick to safely lift rocks and bark. If you find ants, take some time to observe them. If you find someone else that you want to observe, you may take the time to do so. Spend about 10 minutes looking for ants.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Did you find ants? Some other interesting creature? Tell about their habitat: where is it? What is around? What was the creature doing? Do they have a nest? If so, how do you know? Did you notice eggs, larvae, or pupae? Did they carry food? Do you notice how they entered or left their home?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
This week or next is a great opportunity to do an object lesson during the science activity. Questions are embedded in the lesson to help, as needed. If preferred, teachers can instead refer to the Handbook of Nature Study or allow questions to flow naturally between student and teacher.

assignment_description:
Looking for Ants Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

➜ INTRO
Look for ants again this week or return to visit your ants again, if you found some last week.

➜ OBSERVE
Is anything different this week? If you find an ant, carefully take a closer look at its body, being careful not to disturb a nest; you might place an ant in a container temporarily and look at it with a magnifier. How many parts of the body can you count? How many legs? From which parts do the legs come? How many antennae does it have? How does it use its antennae? Spend about 10 minutes looking for/watching ants.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Tell about what you found or what you recorded.

Noticing Bees Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
Look for other insects this week, especially bees.

➜ OBSERVE
Spend about 10 minutes noticing insects. What are they doing? What parts of the body can you see? How would you describe the habitat?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• How is this bee (or other insect) similar to and different from the ant?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
The bees will begin slowing down toward the end of fall. Notice if this is happening, as you might get to see one up close and do an object lesson!

assignment_description:
Noticing Bees Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

➜ INTRO
Look for bees again this week.

➜ OBSERVE
Is anything different this week? Do you find the same type of bee, a different type, or none at all? Some other type of insect? Spend about 10 minutes noticing.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Tell about what you found or what you recorded.

Meeting an Evening Creature
⍞ Materials: Bat Loves the Night

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
One creature that you may have noticed in the evening is the bat. Have you ever seen a bat? If so, what did you notice about them? Briefly describe in words or a picture. Bats need very particular types of habitats. They are also very important helpers to many other creatures. Let's read and find out more.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Bat Loves the Night (whole book)
• What kinds of things do you think the bat needs in its habitat?

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Prompt for General Science in Outdoor Work Quick Link.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Let students enjoy the artwork in this picture book. If you need to take a break and finish it another time, that is fine.

assignment_description:
Noticing Sound Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

➜ INTRO
It can be hard to understand exactly how a bat uses echolocation because we don't share that experience, but we can think about how different sounds sound different.

➜ ACTIVITY
Choose three different objects, such as a penny, a bead, and a small ball of paper. Drop these three objects onto three different surfaces to hear the sound that bounces back to your ear, such as a metal pan, the floor, and a countertop or desk. Do you think you could learn to tell these apart if you couldn't see them? Spend about 10 minutes listening and describing what you hear.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Tell about what you found or what you recorded.

Modeling a Pond Habitat Activity
⍞ Materials: Clay, 12-18" piece of substrate, e.g., cardboard, wood scrap, foam core, found nature objects, optional: craft paint

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
What are some things you have learned about ponds this term? Let's use the last 2 weeks of the term to create a model of a pond habitat. You will use clay to form your pond, paint if desired, and then you can use found nature objects to add the plants and animals that live there.

➜ ACTIVITY
∞ Website Link: Salt Dough Recipe (as an alternative to clay)
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students may need to work on their models in the afternoon if they need extra time.

assignment_description:
Habitats in Winter
⍞ Materials: Winter Sleep

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
Does your home or the way you live change in the winter? Look at the picture on the cover. What do you think happens to the creatures' habitats when the seasons change? How do plants and animals get ready for winter? Briefly describe in words or a picture.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Winter Sleep: A Hibernation Story (whole book)
You may leave the end matter for another time based on student interest.
• Have you ever come across an animal that was sleeping for the winter? If so, what did you notice? What do you notice the non-hibernating animals doing in the winter? What happens to plants in winter?

• NATURE NOTEBOOK

assignment_description:
Looking Closely at Seeds Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
Choose at least one of the collected seeds to draw/paint today. Look closely at it before you begin. What do you notice? How is this seed built? Does it remind you of anything else? Does it give you an idea as to how the seed spreads for planting? Does an animal eat it? Does it hold on to an animal to ride along? Does it ride on the wind?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Tell about what you recorded. Do you know the name of the plant that made this seed?

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Prompt for General Science in Outdoor Work Quick Link.

Thinking about Seeds
⍞ Materials: A Fruit is a Suitcase for Seeds

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
What kinds of fruit do you like to eat? Do you know what kind of plant makes your fruit? Why does the plant make fruit? Look at the picture on the cover. Does your fruit have seeds?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
A Fruit is a Suitcase for Seeds (whole book)
• What do you think happens to seeds in nature when an animal eats the fruit? Do all plants that make seeds package them inside fruit? What about the seed you drew/painted last week? Did it have a fruit?
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
Let students enjoy the artwork in this picture book. If you need to take a break and finish it another time, that is fine.

★ TEACHER NOTE
Students will need a variety of fruits to examine their seeds next week.

assignment_description:
Investigating Fruits Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal, a variety of fruits with seeds

➜ INTRO
Have you ever noticed the different kinds of seeds in the fruit you eat? Let's investigate today!

➜ ACTIVITY
Open a pit fruit (cherry, plum, peach, apricot, avocado), a many-seeded fruit (lemon, melon, apple, pear), a many-many seeded fruit (banana, kiwi, berry), a pea pod, and an ear of corn to find the seeds. Look closely and compare them. How are they different from each other? How are they similar? When you are finished, choose one or more that you would like to plant. Wash them and lay them on a towel to dry.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a nature journal to record what the student noticed.
• Tell about what you found or what you recorded.

Noticing Animal Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

➜ INTRO
Spend about 10 minutes watching animal behavior during this time of seasonal change.

➜ OBSERVE
What are the animals doing? Is anyone visiting your feeder or pile?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• What was different this week compared to previous weeks?

Looking Closely at Seeds Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

➜ INTRO
Draw/paint a seed today.

➜ OBSERVE
Choose another of the collected seeds to draw/paint today. Look closely at it before you begin. What do you notice?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• How does this compare to the seeds obtained from fruits that you eat? Do you think an animal eats this seed? If not, does the seed offer any clues as to how it might spread, so that it can grow? What is its shape? What is its texture?

Planting a Seed Activity
⍞ Materials: Pot, soil, seed, water

➜ ACTIVITY
Plant one or more of the fruit seeds that you saved. Plant it as deep as the seed is wide and give it just enough water to dampen the soil. You will need to continue to keep the soil moist, but not wet, over break. When your seed germinates, place the pot near a window where it will receive sunlight.

assignment_description:
Preparing the Garden Activity
⍞ Materials: Pot with soil or patch of ground, seeds or seedlings, water

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ ACTIVITY
Help prepare a small garden - even a single pot can be a habitat! Plant some seeds/seedlings, or transplant the one that sprouted last term. Remember that you will need to check on it regularly to pull a weed or provide water, as needed.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
It can help to set a reminder in your calendar while students are forming the habit of caring for their garden.

assignment_description:
Bird Watching Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

Today we will notice the birds.

➜ OBSERVE
Spend about 10 minutes bird watching. What colors, markings, and shapes do you notice? What are they doing?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Do you notice anyone already familiar to you? Do you notice anyone new?

Critter Investigation Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal, garden trowel, piece of ground to dig

➜ INTRO
We have spent time noticing creatures in the sky and on the surface of the earth, but what about under the surface? Does anyone live there?

➜ OBSERVE
Spend about 10 minutes digging in the dirt or leaf litter to see who is busy under the surface. If you see a pill bug, what does it do when you pick it up? If you see a worm, how does it feel in your hand? Who is the first visitor up above the surface?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Tell about what you recorded.

Wildflower Watching Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
Today we will spend about 10 minutes noticing which wildflowers are growing in your yard this season.

➜ OBSERVE
What colors and shapes do you notice? Where are they growing? Describe the land and any plants or animals you notice around them. Compare any wildflowers to those growing in the garden. In what ways are they similar or different?

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Do you notice anyone already familiar to you? Do you notice anyone new?

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Prompt for General Science in Outdoor Work Quick Link.

assignment_description:
Noticing Spiders Activity
⍞ Materials: Nature Journal

PREP: Read Teacher Tip.

➜ INTRO
Look for spiders this week. They may be active in grass or wooded areas or in garages or basements where they overwintered. If you don't come across a spider, then just observe whatever nature has provided today!

➜ OBSERVE
Is this a spider that you have noticed before? How is it similar or different to the one in your book? How many body segments do you see? How many legs does it have? How many eyes does it have? What colors, markings, or textures do you see? Does it have a web? If so, describe the shape and construction of the web.

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Use a Nature Journal to record what the student noticed.
• Tell about what you found or what you recorded.

• NATURE NOTEBOOK
Prompt for General Science in Outdoor Work Quick Link.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER TIP
If you know where a spider has built a web, do an object lesson! If students struggle to find them, it's okay. Just encourage them to pursue the nature mystery - if the spiders aren't where we have looked, where could they be? Why aren't they here?
